texto: I'll turn into a god of pain and disease and build an altar to you from the bones of your murderer. Their suffering will be my first odes, and they will not end until I feel satisfied that even dead, resting wherever you are resting, you can hear the pain of the idiot that thought your death would go unavenged.
explicacao: Citação de Ayize Jama-Everett sobre temas ligados a familia. “I'll turn into a god of pain and disease and build an altar to you from the bones of your murdere…” articula familia, anger, anger-righteousness-vengeance em registro de familia, com relevância para contextos como luto e termino.

frase_original: I'll turn into a god of pain and disease and build an altar to you from the bones of your murderer. Their suffering will be my first odes, and they will not end until I feel satisfied that even dead, resting wherever you are resting, you can hear the pain of the idiot that thought your death would go unavenged.

texto: Times like this, I don't wish for ignorance. I look around and I see the bloated ignorance of the lumpen proletariat: roly-poly, sausage-fingered, ginger-topped fathers of at least two illegitimate children trying to massage the asses of waiflike, peroxide-scarred students who are themselves trying to navigate adulthood with their new-found freedom from outdated parenting.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Ayize Jama-Everett: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “Times like this, I don't wish for ignorance. I look around and I see the bloated ignorance of the…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que familia, ignorance, normality pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de normality e riches.
